howItWorks.intro: This calculator starts from a common infant-feeding guideline — roughly 2.5 ounces of prepared formula per pound of body weight each day — and divides that daily total across a typical number of feeds for your baby's age. The result is a ballpark to sanity-check against, not a target your baby has to hit.
rules: Daily guideline: about 2.5 oz of prepared formula per pound of body weight
Most healthy infants level off around 32 oz/day; more than that is worth a chat with your pediatrician
Feeds per day drop as babies grow: newborns often 8-10, around 3 months 6-8, and 6 months and up 5-6 as solids begin
howItWorks.outro: Every baby is different. Treat the numbers here as a rough map and let your baby's hunger and fullness cues, plus your pediatrician's guidance, do the steering.

faq.question: How much formula does my baby need per day by weight?
faq.answer: A widely used rule of thumb is about 2.5 ounces of prepared formula per pound of body weight over 24 hours. A 10-lb baby lands near 25 oz/day, and a 12-lb baby near 30 oz/day. Most healthy infants settle around 32 oz/day at the top end. These are averages — a baby who wants a little more or less on a given day is completely normal.

faq.question: How many ounces should my baby take per feeding?
faq.answer: Take the estimated daily total and divide it by the number of feeds. For a 10-lb baby at about 25 oz/day across 8 feeds, that is roughly 3 oz per bottle. Younger babies eat smaller amounts more often; older babies take larger bottles less often. Following your baby's appetite at each feed matters more than hitting an exact ounce count.

faq.question: Does the amount change as my baby gets older?
faq.answer: Yes. Intake per pound tends to ease off as babies grow, and the number of feeds drops — from 8-10 a day as a newborn toward 5-6 by around 6 months. Once solid foods enter the picture around 6 months, many babies naturally taper their formula somewhat. Weight, appetite, and growth all shift together, so the daily total is a moving target.

faq.question: When should I call my pediatrician about feeding?
faq.answer: Reach out if your baby seems consistently hungry after full feeds, is regularly taking far less than expected, has fewer than about 6 wet diapers a day, isn't gaining weight as expected, or if you have any worry at all. Your pediatrician knows your baby's history and is the right person to answer specific feeding questions. This tool is general guidance only and is not medical advice.

faq.question: How do I know if my baby is getting enough?
faq.answer: Reassuring signs include steady weight gain, roughly 6 or more wet diapers a day, and a baby who seems content between feeds. Hunger cues (rooting, hands to mouth, fussing) and fullness cues (turning away, slowing down, relaxing) are your best real-time signals. When in doubt, your pediatrician can review growth trends with you.

faq.question: Is it okay to go over the estimated amount?
faq.answer: Occasional variation is normal — appetites swing day to day. That said, consistently pushing well past about 32 oz/day for a young infant is worth raising with your pediatrician rather than treating the calculator's number as a floor or a ceiling. Let your baby's cues lead.

howWeCalculate.intro: The math here is intentionally simple and transparent, so you can see exactly where the estimate comes from rather than trusting a black box.
steps: Multiply your baby's weight in pounds by 2.5 to get an approximate daily ounces target
Cap that daily figure near 32 oz, the level most healthy infants settle around
Pick a typical feed count for the age you entered (more feeds for newborns, fewer as babies grow)
Divide the daily total by the feed count to estimate a per-feeding amount
howWeCalculate.disclaimer: These figures are a general estimate, not medical advice. Babies vary widely, and this cannot account for your baby's specific health, prematurity, growth pattern, or appetite. Always follow your pediatrician's guidance and your baby's hunger and fullness cues over any number shown here.

whenToUse.intro: This is handy for parents who want a quick reference point while feeding routines are still taking shape.
scenarios: Planning how much formula to prepare and pack for the day or for a caregiver
Checking whether your baby's usual intake falls in a typical range for their weight and age
Estimating how many bottles and roughly what size to expect across a day
Getting a rough sense of how much formula you'll go through so you can restock in time
Framing a specific question to bring to your next pediatrician visit

sections.title: What This Calculator Estimates
sections.body: Enter your baby's weight and age, and this tool estimates a daily formula amount, a rough per-feeding size, and how many feeds that might look like across a day. It leans on a common infant-feeding guideline of about 2.5 ounces of prepared formula per pound of body weight, with most healthy babies leveling off near 32 ounces a day. The goal is a friendly reference point while you're finding your baby's rhythm — not a rule your baby has to follow to the ounce.

sections.title: A Worked Example
sections.body: Say your baby weighs 10 pounds. Multiply 10 by 2.5 and you get about 25 ounces of formula over a full day. Spread across roughly 8 feeds, that works out to around 3 ounces per bottle. A heavier baby lands higher, and a newborn feeding 10 times a day takes smaller bottles more often. These are averages: some days your baby drinks a bit more, some days a bit less, and both are normal. Watch what your baby actually does at the breast or bottle rather than chasing the exact figure.

sections.title: General Guidance, Not Medical Advice
sections.body: Feeding is one of the areas where every baby writes their own rules. Prematurity, growth spurts, health conditions, and plain old appetite all shift how much a baby needs, and no calculator can see those. Use this as a starting conversation point, lean on your baby's hunger and fullness cues in the moment, and let your pediatrician have the final word on anything specific to your child.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Baby's Weight (lbs)
howToSteps.description: Type in your baby's most recent weight in pounds. A recent number from a check-up or home scale gives the closest estimate.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Baby's Age (months)
howToSteps.description: Add your baby's age in months. Age helps set a typical number of feeds per day, since newborns eat more often than older infants.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Press Calculate to see an estimated daily amount, a rough per-feeding size, and a typical feed count appear below.

howToSteps.title: Read It as a Guide
howToSteps.description: Use the results as a ballpark to compare against your baby's usual intake. Let hunger and fullness cues, and your pediatrician, guide the actual amounts.

tips: Think of the daily number as a range, not a fixed target — appetites naturally swing from day to day.
Watch your baby's cues at each feed: rooting and hands-to-mouth mean hungry, while turning away or slowing down means done.
Update the weight you enter after check-ups, since the estimate shifts as your baby grows.
Roughly 6 or more wet diapers a day and steady weight gain are reassuring signs feeding is going well.
If your baby seems consistently hungrier or takes much less than expected, jot it down and mention it to your pediatrician.
